### Install dependencies and run Bun examples

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/bench/README.md

Install npm dependencies and execute various example scripts demonstrating Bun features including FFI, logging, compression, async operations, and SQLite integration.

```bash
npm install

bun run ffi
bun run log
bun run gzip
bun run async
bun run sqlite
```

--------------------------------

### Setup benchmark fixtures for Bun and Vitest

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/bench/test/README.md

Prepare the environment by installing dependencies and running setup scripts to generate the test suites.

```sh
cd bench/test
bun install                       # for vitest
bun parallel/setup.ts
bun isolate-cache/setup.ts
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Setup Scripts in Local Template package.json

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/templating/create.mdx

Add 'preinstall' and 'postinstall' scripts within the 'bun-create' section of your local template's 'package.json'. These scripts run before and after dependency installation.

```json
{
  "name": "@bun-examples/simplereact",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "main": "index.js",
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "^17.0.2",
    "react-dom": "^17.0.2"
  },
  "bun-create": {
    "preinstall": "echo 'Installing...'", // a single command
    "postinstall": ["echo 'Done!'"], // an array of commands
    "start": "bun run echo 'Hello world!'"
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

### Clone and Set Up bun-lambda Project

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/packages/bun-lambda/README.md

This sequence of shell commands clones the bun repository, checks out the `packages/bun-lambda` sparse directory, navigates into it, installs dependencies, and publishes the Lambda layer to AWS. This is the initial setup required to get the bun-lambda project running locally and deploy the layer.

```sh
git clone --filter=blob:none --sparse https://github.com/oven-sh/bun.git
git -C bun sparse-checkout set packages/bun-lambda
cd bun/packages/bun-lambda
bun install
bun run publish-layer
```

--------------------------------

### Install dependencies and run development server with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/solidstart.mdx

Installs project dependencies using Bun and starts the development server. The dev server runs on localhost:3000 with hot-reload enabled for automatic updates when source files change.

```shell
cd my-app
bun install
bun dev
```

--------------------------------

### Install dependencies with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/bench/expect-to-equal/README.md

Installs project dependencies using the Bun package manager. This is the initial setup step required before running any tests.

```bash
bun install
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize Qwik Application with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/qwik.mdx

This command initializes a new Qwik application using Bun. It leverages `bunx create-qwik`, which automatically detects Bun and installs dependencies using it. The process guides the user through selecting a project name, starter template, and options for dependency installation and Git initialization, resulting in a ready-to-use Qwik project directory.

```sh
bun create qwik
```

--------------------------------

### Bun Test: Multi-File Project Setup

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/CLAUDE.md

This example demonstrates testing a project with multiple files by creating a temporary directory using tempDir and spawning Bun to execute the main entry point. Useful for integration tests involving module imports.

```typescript
import { test, expect } from "bun:test";
import { bunEnv, bunExe, normalizeBunSnapshot, tempDir } from "harness";

test("(multi-file test) my feature", async () => {
  // Create temp directory with test files
  using dir = tempDir("test-prefix", {
    "index.js": `import { foo } from "./foo.ts"; foo();`,
    "foo.ts": `export function foo() { console.log("foo"); }`,
  });

  // Spawn Bun process
  await using proc = Bun.spawn({
    cmd: [bunExe(), "index.js"],
    env: bunEnv,
    cwd: String(dir),
    stderr: "pipe",
  });

  const [stdout, stderr, exitCode] = await Promise.all([
    proc.stdout.text(),
    proc.stderr.text(),
    proc.exited,
  ]);

  // Prefer snapshot tests over expect(stdout).toBe("hello\n");
  expect(normalizeBunSnapshot(stdout, dir)).toMatchInlineSnapshot(`"hello"`);

  // Assert the exit code last. This gives you a more useful error message on test failure.
  expect(exitCode).toBe(0);
});
```

--------------------------------

### Complete uWS Server Setup with GET Route and Event Loop

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/packages/bun-uws/misc/READMORE.md

Demonstrates a minimal but complete uWS application that defines a GET route handler, listens on port 9001, and enters the event loop with App.run(). The blocking run() call returns when all async work completes and sockets are closed, allowing graceful shutdown with automatic memory cleanup via RAII.

```c++
int main() {
    uWS::App().get("/*", [](auto *res, auto *req) {
        res->end("Hello World!");
    }).listen(9001, [](auto *listenSocket) {
        if (listenSocket) {
            std::cout << "Listening for connections..." << std::endl;
        }
    }).run();

    std::cout << "Shoot! We failed to listen and the App fell through, exiting now!" << std::endl;
}
```

--------------------------------

### onStart Hook

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/esbuild.mdx

Hook that is called when the bundle starts. Use this to perform initialization tasks or setup operations before bundling begins.

```APIDOC
## onStart Hook

### Description
Called when the bundle starts. Use this hook to perform initialization tasks or setup operations before the bundling process begins.

### Method
builder.onStart(callback: () => void): void

### Callback
- No parameters
- No return value

### Example
```typescript
builder.onStart(() => {
  console.log("Bundle starting...");
});
```
```

--------------------------------

### Run Development Server with npm

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/third_party/pnpm/install_fixture/README.md

Start the development server in development mode. The application will be accessible at http://localhost:5173 in the browser with hot module reloading enabled.

```bash
npm run dev
```

### Manually Install Required Packages for Bun Secrets API Tests

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This command sequence updates package lists and installs the essential dependencies (`libsecret-1-dev`, `gnome-keyring`, `dbus-x11`) required for `Bun.secrets` API functionality on Ubuntu/Debian systems, as part of a manual CI setup.

```bash
apt-get update && apt-get install -y libsecret-1-dev gnome-keyring dbus-x11
```

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/prisma-postgres.mdx

Create a new directory and install the Prisma CLI, Prisma Client, and Accelerate extension.

```bash
mkdir prisma-postgres-app
cd prisma-postgres-app
bun init
```

```bash
bun add -d prisma
bun add @prisma/client @prisma/extension-accelerate
```

### Run Bun Secrets Tests in GitHub Actions (Explicit Keyring Setup)

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This GitHub Actions workflow explicitly installs keyring packages (`libsecret-1-dev`, `gnome-keyring`, `dbus-x11`) using `apt-get` to ensure all dependencies for Bun's secrets API are met. After installation, it executes the Bun secrets tests.

```yaml
- name: Install keyring packages  
  run: |
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y libsecret-1-dev gnome-keyring dbus-x11

- name: Run secrets tests
  run: bun test test/js/bun/secrets.test.ts
```

--------------------------------

### Customize Usage Description with .usage()

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

Allows customization of the usage description displayed in the first line of help output. Takes a string parameter describing the command syntax and options.

```javascript
program
  .name("my-command")
  .usage("[global options] command");
```

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/integration/next-pages/README.md

Commands to start the Next.js development server using npm, yarn, pnpm, or bun. The server runs on http://localhost:3000 and supports hot-reloading for file changes.

```bash
npm run dev
```

```bash
yarn dev
```

```bash
pnpm dev
```

```bash
bun dev
```

### Install Bun CLI on macOS, Linux, or WSL

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/web/fetch/fixture.html

This command installs the Bun command-line interface (CLI) on macOS (x64 & Silicon), Linux (x64), and Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL). It fetches an installation script from the official Bun website and executes it using bash, providing a quick way to get started with Bun.

```bash
curl https://bun.sh/install | bash
```

--------------------------------

### Basic Bun.serve setup with routing

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/http/server.mdx

This snippet demonstrates how to set up a basic HTTP server with `Bun.serve`, including static, dynamic, method-specific, wildcard, redirect, and file serving routes.

```ts
const server = Bun.serve({
  // `routes` requires Bun v1.2.3+
  routes: {
    // Static routes
    "/api/status": new Response("OK"),

    // Dynamic routes
    "/users/:id": req => {
      return new Response(`Hello User ${req.params.id}!`);
    },

    // Per-HTTP method handlers
    "/api/posts": {
      GET: () => new Response("List posts"),
      POST: async req => {
        const body = await req.json();
        return Response.json({ created: true, ...body });
      }
    },

    // Wildcard route for all routes that start with "/api/" and aren't otherwise matched
    "/api/*": Response.json({ message: "Not found" }, { status: 404 }),

    // Redirect from /blog/hello to /blog/hello/world
    "/blog/hello": Response.redirect("/blog/hello/world"),

    // Serve a file by lazily loading it into memory
    "/favicon.ico": Bun.file("./favicon.ico")
  },

  // (optional) fallback for unmatched routes:
  // Required if Bun's version < 1.2.3
  fetch(req) {
    return new Response("Not Found", { status: 404 });
  }
});

console.log(`Server running at ${server.url}`);
```

--------------------------------

### Run Bun Secrets API Tests Automatically in CI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This command executes the `Bun.secrets` API tests, leveraging the automatic CI detection and setup features. It handles package installation, keyring configuration, and service initialization on supported Linux environments.

```bash
bun test test/js/bun/secrets.test.ts
```

```sh
mkdir mongoose-app

bun init

bun add mongoose
```

### Example Output: Commander.js Showing Help After Error

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This console output illustrates the behavior when `showHelpAfterError()` is enabled in Commander.js. Upon encountering an unknown option, an error message is displayed, followed by the custom hint configured to guide the user towards the help documentation.

```console
$ pizza --unknown
error: unknown option '--unknown'
(add --help for additional information)
```

--------------------------------

### Complete Server Example

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/packages/bun-uws/misc/READMORE.md

A complete example demonstrating how to create an HTTP server, define routes, listen on a port, and run the event loop using the uWS framework.

```APIDOC
## Complete uWS Server Setup

### Description
Full example of initializing a uWS application with route handling, port listening, and event loop execution.

### Code Example
```cpp
int main() {
    uWS::App().get("/*", [](auto *res, auto *req) {
        res->end("Hello World!");
    }).listen(9001, [](auto *listenSocket) {
        if (listenSocket) {
            std::cout << "Listening for connections..." << std::endl;
        }
    }).run();

    std::cout << "Shoot! We failed to listen and the App fell through, exiting now!" << std::endl;
}
```

### Components
1. **Route Definition** - `.get("/*", callback)` handles all GET requests
2. **Port Binding** - `.listen(9001, callback)` binds to port 9001
3. **Event Loop** - `.run()` starts the blocking event loop
4. **Method Chaining** - All methods return App instance for chaining

### Execution Flow
1. App instance created
2. Route handler registered
3. Server binds to port 9001
4. Event loop starts and blocks
5. Server processes incoming requests
6. Loop exits when all resources cleaned up
```

--------------------------------

### Install Core Packages for Bun Secrets API on Ubuntu/Debian

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This `apt-get` command installs the fundamental packages necessary for the `Bun.secrets` API to interact with the system keyring on Ubuntu/Debian. It includes the libsecret development headers, the GNOME Keyring daemon, and D-Bus X11 integration.

```bash
apt-get install -y \
  libsecret-1-dev \
  gnome-keyring \
  dbus-x11
```

--------------------------------

### Run Bun Secrets Tests in Docker

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This Dockerfile snippet first installs required keyring dependencies (`libsecret-1-dev`, `gnome-keyring`, `dbus-x11`) to support Bun's secrets API. Following the dependency installation, it executes the Bun secrets tests within the Docker image.

```dockerfile
# Optional: pre-install packages for faster test startup
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
    libsecret-1-dev \
    gnome-keyring \
    dbus-x11

# Run test normally - setup is automatic
RUN bun test test/js/bun/secrets.test.ts
```

Create a new project directory and initialize it with Bun.

```sh
mkdir my-edgedb-app
cd my-edgedb-app
bun init -y
```

### Example Output of Custom Help Text in Commander.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This text output shows the result of adding custom help text to a Commander.js program. The `Example call:` section, defined using `addHelpText('after', ...)`, is appended to the standard help output, enhancing the user's understanding.

```Text
Usage: custom-help [options]

Options:
  -f, --foo   enable some foo
  -h, --help  display help for command

Example call:
  $ custom-help --help
```

### Add Custom Help Text to Commander.js Output

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This JavaScript snippet demonstrates how to add custom text to the auto-generated help output of a Commander.js program using `addHelpText()`. The example adds an 'after' position text, which appears after the built-in help options, providing additional usage examples.

```js
program
  .option('-f, --foo', 'enable some foo');

program.addHelpText('after', `

Example call:
  $ custom-help --help`);
```

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/quickstart.mdx

Scaffold a new project directory and select a template using the interactive initialization command.

```bash
bun init my-app
```

```bash
bun init my-app
```

```txt
✓ Select a project template: Blank

- .gitignore
- CLAUDE.md
- .cursor/rules/use-bun-instead-of-node-vite-npm-pnpm.mdc -> CLAUDE.md
- index.ts
- tsconfig.json (for editor autocomplete)
- README.md
```

--------------------------------

### Create Multi-Command CLI with Subcommands in Node.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

Advanced Commander.js example creating a named CLI program with version, description, and subcommands. Demonstrates command definition with arguments, options, descriptions, and action handlers for processing user input.

```javascript
const { Command } = require('commander');
const program = new Command();

program
  .name('string-util')
  .description('CLI to some JavaScript string utilities')
  .version('0.8.0');

program.command('split')
  .description('Split a string into substrings and display as an array')
  .argument('<string>', 'string to split')
  .option('--first', 'display just the first substring')
  .option('-s, --separator <char>', 'separator character', ',')
  .action((str, options) => {
    const limit = options.first ? 1 : undefined;
    console.log(str.split(options.separator, limit));
  });

program.parse();
```

--------------------------------

### Add Start Script to package.json

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/quickstart.mdx

Illustrates how to define a 'start' script in `package.json` to run the main application file, enabling easier execution.

```json
{
  "name": "quickstart",
  "module": "index.ts",
  "type": "module",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": { 
    "start": "bun run index.ts" 
  }, 
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/bun": "latest"
  },
  "peerDependencies": {
    "typescript": "^5"
  }
}
```

--------------------------------

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/templating/init.mdx

Use `bun init` followed by a directory name to start an interactive project scaffolding process.

```bash
bun init my-app
```

### Example Output: Commander.js Without Spelling Suggestions

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This console output demonstrates the effect of disabling spelling suggestions in Commander.js. When an unknown, misspelled option is provided, only the error message is displayed, without the 'Did you mean?' suggestion that would normally appear.

```console
$ pizza --hepl
error: unknown option '--hepl'
```

--------------------------------

### Access Help for Commander.js Subcommands via Shell

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This shell script illustrates different ways to invoke help for a Commander.js program and its subcommands. It shows that `help` and `--help` can be used interchangeably for the main command, and also how to get help for specific subcommands.

```sh
shell help
shell --help

shell help spawn
shell spawn --help
```

--------------------------------

### Complete Bun.build() compilation example with plugin

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/executables.mdx

Full example demonstrating Bun.build() with compile options, plugin integration, minification, sourcemaps, bytecode, environment variables, and success validation.

```typescript
import type { BunPlugin } from "bun";

const myPlugin: BunPlugin = {
  name: "my-plugin",
  setup(build) {
    // Plugin implementation
  },
};

const result = await Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ["./src/cli.ts"],
  compile: {
    target: "bun-linux-x64",
    outfile: "./dist/mycli",
    execArgv: ["--smol"],
    autoloadDotenv: false,
    autoloadBunfig: false,
  },
  minify: true,
  sourcemap: "linked",
  bytecode: true,
  define: {
    "process.env.NODE_ENV": JSON.stringify("production"),
    VERSION: JSON.stringify("1.0.0"),
  },
  plugins: [myPlugin],
});

if (result.success) {
  console.log("Build successful:", result.outputs[0].path);
}
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize a Bun Project

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/discordjs.mdx

Create a new directory and initialize it as a Bun project using `bun init`.

```sh
mkdir my-bot
cd my-bot
bun init
```

--------------------------------

### Define Negatable Boolean Options in CLI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

Demonstrates negatable boolean options using the `--no-` prefix to set option values to false. This allows defining options that can be explicitly disabled. The example shows how to handle both regular and negatable options together.

```javascript
program
  .option('--no-sauce', 'Remove sauce')
  .option('--cheese <flavour>', 'cheese flavour', 'mozzarella')
  .option('--no-cheese', 'plain with no cheese')
  .parse();

const options = program.opts();
const sauceStr = options.sauce ? 'sauce' : 'no sauce';
const cheeseStr = (options.cheese === false) ? 'no cheese' : `${options.cheese} cheese`;
console.log(`You ordered a pizza with ${sauceStr} and ${cheeseStr}`);
```

--------------------------------

### Parse Command-Line Arguments with Options in Node.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

Basic Commander.js setup that defines boolean and value options, then parses command-line arguments. The program splits a string using a specified separator character. Demonstrates option parsing and accessing parsed values via program.opts().

```javascript
const { program } = require('commander');

program
  .option('--first')
  .option('-s, --separator <char>');

program.parse();

const options = program.opts();
const limit = options.first ? 1 : undefined;
console.log(program.args[0].split(options.separator, limit));
```

--------------------------------

### Install NAPI CLI and create native plugin project

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/plugins.mdx

Set up the NAPI CLI tool and initialize a new native plugin project. Run these commands to prepare the Rust environment for native plugin development.

```bash
bun add -g @napi-rs/cli
napi new
```

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/quickstart.mdx

Runs the 'start' script defined in `package.json` using `bun run`. The output shows the server successfully starting.

```bash
bun run start
```

```txt
Listening on http://localhost:3000
```

### Set up Bun project with Drizzle and Neon serverless driver

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/neon-drizzle.mdx

Initialize a new Bun project and install necessary dependencies for Drizzle ORM and the Neon serverless driver.

```sh
mkdir bun-drizzle-neon
cd bun-drizzle-neon
bun init -y
bun add drizzle-orm @neondatabase/serverless
bun add -D drizzle-kit
```

--------------------------------

### Delete Bun Secret by Setting Empty String Value (TypeScript)

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This TypeScript example illustrates an alternative method to delete a stored credential using `Bun.secrets.set()`. By providing an empty string as the `value`, the credential associated with the specified service and name will be removed, offering simplified set/delete operations.

```typescript
// These are equivalent:
await Bun.secrets.delete({ service: "myapp", name: "token" });
await Bun.secrets.set({ service: "myapp", name: "token", value: "" });
```

--------------------------------

### Preconnect to a Host at Startup via CLI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/networking/fetch.mdx

Use the `--fetch-preconnect` CLI flag with `bun` to preconnect to a specified host when the application starts, similar to `<link rel="preconnect">` in HTML.

```sh
bun --fetch-preconnect https://bun.com ./my-script.ts
```

--------------------------------

### Install Bun using npm

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/installation.mdx

Install Bun globally using npm. This is a convenient way to get Bun if you already have Node.js and npm set up.

```bash
npm install -g bun # the last `npm` command you'll ever need
```

--------------------------------

### Define an HTTP route and start Elysia server

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/elysia.mdx

Initializes an Elysia instance, defines a root GET route, and starts listening on port 8080.

```typescript
import { Elysia } from "elysia";

const app = new Elysia().get("/", () => "Hello Elysia").listen(8080);

console.log(`🦺 Elysia is running at on port ${app.server?.port}...`);
```

--------------------------------

### Set up a StricJS project with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/stric.mdx

Initialize a new Bun project and add the necessary StricJS router and utility packages.

```bash
mkdir myapp
cd myapp
bun init
bun add @stricjs/router @stricjs/utils
```

--------------------------------

### Implement Life Cycle Hooks in Commander.js Commands

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This JavaScript example illustrates how to add callback hooks to Commander.js commands for various life cycle events, such as 'preAction'. Hooks can be used to perform actions or log information before a command's action handler is executed, and they can be asynchronous, requiring `parseAsync`.

```js
program
  .option('-t, --trace', 'display trace statements for commands')
  .hook('preAction', (thisCommand, actionCommand) => {
    if (thisCommand.opts().trace) {
      console.log(`About to call action handler for subcommand: ${actionCommand.name()}`);
      console.log('arguments: %O', actionCommand.args);
      console.log('options: %o', actionCommand.opts());
    }
  });
```

--------------------------------

### Define Boolean and Value Options in CLI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

Demonstrates how to define basic boolean options (flags) and options that accept values using angle brackets. Boolean options are undefined unless specified, while value options require an argument. The example shows parsing arguments and accessing options through program.opts().

```javascript
program
  .option('-d, --debug', 'output extra debugging')
  .option('-s, --small', 'small pizza size')
  .option('-p, --pizza-type <type>', 'flavour of pizza');

program.parse(process.argv);

const options = program.opts();
if (options.debug) console.log(options);
console.log('pizza details:');
if (options.small) console.log('- small pizza size');
if (options.pizzaType) console.log(`- ${options.pizzaType}`);
```

--------------------------------

### Bun.build() Configuration Options

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/esbuild.mdx

Reference guide for Bun.build() configuration parameters and their equivalents to esbuild.build(). This table documents which esbuild options are supported, partially supported, or unsupported in Bun, along with implementation notes.

```APIDOC
## Bun.build() Configuration Reference

### Overview
Bun.build() is the bundling API for Bun. While it shares similarities with esbuild.build(), there are key differences in parameter support and behavior.

### Configuration Parameters

#### absWorkingDir
- **esbuild equivalent**: `absWorkingDir`
- **Bun support**: Not configurable
- **Notes**: Always set to `process.cwd()`

#### alias
- **esbuild equivalent**: `alias`
- **Bun support**: Not supported
- **Notes**: Path aliasing is not available in Bun.build()

#### allowOverwrite
- **esbuild equivalent**: `allowOverwrite`
- **Bun support**: Not configurable
- **Notes**: Always false

#### assetNames
- **esbuild equivalent**: `assetNames`
- **Bun support**: `naming.asset`
- **Notes**: Uses same templating syntax as esbuild, but `[ext]` must be included explicitly
- **Example**:
```typescript
Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ["./index.tsx"],
  naming: {
    asset: "[name].[ext]",
  },
});
```

#### banner
- **esbuild equivalent**: `banner`
- **Bun support**: Not supported
- **Notes**: Banner injection is not available in Bun.build()

#### bundle
- **esbuild equivalent**: `bundle`
- **Bun support**: Not configurable
- **Notes**: Always true. Use `Bun.Transpiler` to transpile without bundling.

#### charset
- **esbuild equivalent**: `charset`
- **Bun support**: Not supported
- **Notes**: Character set configuration is not available in Bun.build()
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Advanced Command-line Arguments with Commander.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This example demonstrates using `commander.Argument` explicitly to configure arguments with advanced features. It shows how to define argument choices for validation (e.g., 'small', 'medium', 'large') and how to set a default value along with a descriptive text for that default, providing more control over argument behavior.

```javascript
program
  .addArgument(new commander.Argument('<drink-size>', 'drink cup size').choices(['small', 'medium', 'large']))
  .addArgument(new commander.Argument('[timeout]', 'timeout in seconds').default(60, 'one minute'))
```

--------------------------------

### Bun.serve() - Basic Routing Setup

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/http/routing.mdx

Configure basic routes in Bun.serve() with static paths and a fallback fetch handler for unmatched requests. Routes are defined as an object mapping paths to handler functions that return Response objects.

```APIDOC
## Bun.serve()

### Description
Initialize an HTTP server with route handlers and optional fallback fetch handler.

### Method
Configuration API

### Parameters

#### Configuration Object
- **routes** (object) - Optional - Object mapping route paths to handler functions or Response objects
- **fetch** (function) - Optional - Fallback handler for unmatched routes. Receives a Request and returns a Response or Promise<Response>
- **port** (number) - Optional - Port to listen on

### Route Handler Signature
```
(request: BunRequest) => Response | Promise<Response>
```

### BunRequest Interface
- **params** (Record<string, string>) - Route parameters extracted from the path
- **cookies** (CookieMap) - Parsed cookies from the request
- Extends standard Web API Request

### Request Example
```ts
Bun.serve({
  routes: {
    "/": () => new Response("Home"),
    "/api": () => Response.json({ success: true }),
    "/users": async () => Response.json({ users: [] })
  },
  fetch() {
    return new Response("Unmatched route");
  }
});
```

### Response
Returns a server object that listens for HTTP requests and dispatches them to matching route handlers.
```

--------------------------------

### GET /api/*

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/http/server.mdx

A wildcard route that catches any unmatched requests starting with '/api/'.

```APIDOC
## GET /api/*

### Description
Serves as a fallback for any routes under `/api/` that are not explicitly matched.

### Method
GET

### Endpoint
/api/*

### Response
#### Error Response (404)
- **message** (string) - A message indicating the resource was not found.

#### Response Example
{
  "message": "Not found"
}
```

--------------------------------

### Basic route setup with Bun.serve

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/http/routing.mdx

Define static and dynamic routes using the routes property. Unmatched requests fall through to the fetch handler.

```typescript
Bun.serve({
  routes: {
    "/": () => new Response("Home"),
    "/api": () => Response.json({ success: true }),
    "/users": async () => Response.json({ users: [] }),
  },
  fetch() {
    return new Response("Unmatched route");
  },
});
```

--------------------------------

### Stack trace output example from Error.captureStackTrace

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/debugger.mdx

Example output showing how Error.captureStackTrace modifies the stack trace to start from the specified function boundary instead of the actual throw point.

```text
Error: here!
    at myInner (file.js:4:15)
    at fn (file.js:8:5)
    at module code (file.js:17:1)
    at moduleEvaluation (native)
    at moduleEvaluation (native)
    at <anonymous> (native)

-- captureStackTrace --

Error: here!
    at module code (file.js:17:1)
    at moduleEvaluation (native)
    at moduleEvaluation (native)
    at <anonymous> (native)
```

--------------------------------

### Start development server with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/src/runtime/cli/init/README2.default.md

Start a development server with hot-reload capabilities for local development and testing.

```bash
bun dev
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Bun global installation paths

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/pm/cli/add.mdx

Provides an example of `bunfig.toml` configuration to customize the directories where `bun add --global` installs packages and links their binaries. This allows users to control the global installation behavior of Bun.

```toml
[install]
# where `bun add --global` installs packages
globalDir = "~/.bun/install/global"

# where globally-installed package bins are linked
globalBinDir = "~/.bun/bin"
```

--------------------------------

### Define Variadic Command-line Arguments in Commander.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This example illustrates how to define a variadic argument, which collects multiple values into an array. By appending `...` to the argument name (e.g., `<dirs...>`), the last argument can accept an indefinite number of values. These values are then passed as an array to the action handler, allowing for operations on multiple items.

```javascript
program
  .version('0.1.0')
  .command('rmdir')
  .argument('<dirs...>')
  .action(function (dirs) {
    dirs.forEach((dir) => {
      console.log('rmdir %s', dir);
    });
  });
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize Bun Project and Install Drizzle ORM

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/drizzle.mdx

Use `bun init` to create a new project and `bun add` to install Drizzle ORM and Drizzle Kit dependencies.

```sh
bun init -y
bun add drizzle-orm
bun add -D drizzle-kit
```

--------------------------------

### Bun.serve - HTTP Server

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/bun-apis.mdx

Start an HTTP server using Bun's native server API. This is the canonical way to create a web server in Bun, accepting a fetch handler that processes incoming requests and returns responses.

```APIDOC
## Bun.serve

### Description
Start an HTTP server with a fetch handler to process incoming requests.

### Method
Function call

### Signature
```typescript
Bun.serve(options: ServeOptions): Server
```

### Parameters
- **fetch** (function) - Required - Handler function that receives a Request and returns a Response

### Request Example
```typescript
Bun.serve({
  fetch(req: Request) {
    return new Response("Success!");
  },
});
```
```

--------------------------------

### Bun Install Disabling Lifecycle Script Optimizations

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/pm/cli/install.mdx

Provides examples of environment variables to disable Bun's automatic optimizations for postinstall scripts. This offers fine-grained control over the installation process, useful for specific build environments or debugging.

```bash
BUN_FEATURE_FLAG_DISABLE_NATIVE_DEPENDENCY_LINKER=1 bun install
BUN_FEATURE_FLAG_DISABLE_IGNORE_SCRIPTS=1 bun install
```

--------------------------------

### Setup Bun in GitHub Actions Workflow

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/pm/cli/install.mdx

Configure a GitHub Actions workflow to install Bun and run build commands. Uses the official oven-sh/setup-bun action to set up Bun v2, install dependencies, and execute build scripts in an Ubuntu environment.

```yaml
name: bun-types
jobs:
  build:
    name: build-app
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repo
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install bun
        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: bun install
      - name: Build app
        run: bun run build
```

--------------------------------

### Comprehensive examples of bun info command usage

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/pm/cli/info.mdx

This section provides various examples demonstrating how to use `bun info` to view basic package details, specific versions, all available versions, dependencies, homepage URLs, and to retrieve output in JSON format.

```bash
# View basic package information
bun info is-number

# View a specific version
bun info is-number@7.0.0

# View all available versions
bun info is-number versions

# View package dependencies
bun info express dependencies

# View package homepage
bun info lodash homepage

# Get JSON output
bun info react --json
```

--------------------------------

### Start Production Remix App with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/remix.mdx

After building the application, run `bun start` from the project root to launch the production server. This command typically uses `remix-serve` to host the compiled application, making it accessible at `http://localhost:3000`.

```sh
bun start
```

```txt
$ remix-serve ./build/index.js
[remix-serve] http://localhost:3000 (http://192.168.86.237:3000)
```

--------------------------------

### Define Asynchronous Action Handlers in Commander.js

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This example illustrates how to define an `async` function as an action handler for commands. This is useful when command logic involves asynchronous operations, such as network requests or file I/O. To properly handle asynchronous action handlers, the command parsing must also be asynchronous, achieved by calling `program.parseAsync()` instead of `program.parse()`.

```javascript
async function run() { /* code goes here */ }

async function main() {
  program
    .command('run')
    .action(run);
  await program.parseAsync(process.argv);
}
```

--------------------------------

### Navigate to project and install dependencies with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/vite.mdx

After project creation, these commands navigate into the new project directory and install all required dependencies using Bun's package manager, `bun install`.

```bash
cd my-app
bun install
```

--------------------------------

### Create TanStack Start App with Bun CLI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/tanstack-start.mdx

This command uses the `@tanstack/cli` to scaffold a new TanStack Start project. It leverages `bunx` to execute the CLI tool without global installation, creating a project directory named `my-tanstack-app`.

```sh
bunx @tanstack/cli create my-tanstack-app
```

--------------------------------

### Serve Static and File Routes in Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/http/routing.mdx

Demonstrates how to configure Bun.serve with both static routes (content buffered at startup) and file routes (content read per request) for different file serving behaviors.

```ts
Bun.serve({
  routes: {
    // Static route - content is buffered in memory at startup
    "/logo.png": new Response(await Bun.file("./logo.png").bytes()),

    // File route - content is read from filesystem on each request
    "/download.zip": new Response(Bun.file("./download.zip")),
  }
});
```

--------------------------------

### Import aliased package in TypeScript

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/install/npm-alias.mdx

Import and use a package that was installed under an alias. After installing with a custom alias, you can import the package using the alias name instead of the original package name. This example imports the zod package that was aliased as my-custom-name.

```typescript
import { z } from "my-custom-name";

z.string();
```

--------------------------------

### Install Prisma dependencies

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/prisma.mdx

Install Prisma CLI, Prisma Client, and LibSQL adapter for database access.

```bash
bun add -d prisma
bun add @prisma/client @prisma/adapter-libsql
```

--------------------------------

### Access Arguments, Options, and Command Object in Commander.js Action Handler

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This example shows how the action handler receives command arguments, parsed options, and the command object itself as parameters. It demonstrates accessing argument values directly, retrieving option values (e.g., `options.title`), and using the command object (e.g., `command.name()`) for more contextual logic, such as conditional debugging output.

```javascript
program
  .argument('<name>')
  .option('-t, --title <honorific>', 'title to use before name')
  .option('-d, --debug', 'display some debugging')
  .action((name, options, command) => {
    if (options.debug) {
      console.error('Called %s with options %o', command.name(), options);
    }
    const title = options.title ? `${options.title} ` : '';
    console.log(`Thank-you ${title}${name}`);
  });
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies with npm, pnpm, or yarn

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/third_party/pnpm/install_fixture/README.md

Install project dependencies using one of the supported package managers. This command sets up all required packages for the Solid.js project to run locally.

```bash
npm install
```

```bash
pnpm install
```

```bash
yarn install
```

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/hono.mdx

These commands navigate into the newly created project directory (`myapp`) and then use `bun install` to download and install all required project dependencies. This prepares the Hono application for development or production use.

```sh
cd myapp
bun install
```

### Initialize Gel project

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/gel.mdx

Initialize a Gel instance for the project and view the configuration output.

```sh
gel project init
```

```txt
No `gel.toml` found in `/Users/colinmcd94/Documents/bun/fun/examples/my-gel-app` or above
Do you want to initialize a new project? [Y/n]
> Y
Specify the name of Gel instance to use with this project [default: my_gel_app]:
> my_gel_app
Checking Gel versions...
Specify the version of Gel to use with this project [default: x.y]:
> x.y
┌─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project directory   │ /Users/colinmcd94/Documents/bun/fun/examples/my-gel-app         │
│ Project config      │ /Users/colinmcd94/Documents/bun/fun/examples/my-gel-app/gel.toml│
│ Schema dir (empty)  │ /Users/colinmcd94/Documents/bun/fun/examples/my-gel-app/dbschema│
│ Installation method │ portable package                                                 │
│ Version             │ x.y+6d5921b                                                      │
│ Instance name       │ my_gel_app                                                       │
└─────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Version x.y+6d5921b is already downloaded
Initializing Gel instance...
Applying migrations...
Everything is up to date. Revision initial
Project initialized.
To connect to my_gel_app, run `gel`
```

--------------------------------

### Declare and Use Variadic Options in JavaScript CLI with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/node/test/fixtures/postject-copy/node_modules/commander/Readme.md

This snippet demonstrates how to define variadic command-line options using `program.option()` in JavaScript, allowing multiple arguments for a single option. It shows examples of how these options are parsed into an array, how remaining arguments are handled, and the effect of the `--` separator. This functionality is useful for commands that accept a variable number of inputs for a specific flag.

```javascript
program
  .option('-n, --number <numbers...>', 'specify numbers')
  .option('-l, --letter [letters...]', 'specify letters');

program.parse();

console.log('Options: ', program.opts());
console.log('Remaining arguments: ', program.args);
```

```console
$ collect -n 1 2 3 --letter a b c
Options:  { number: [ '1', '2', '3' ], letter: [ 'a', 'b', 'c' ] }
Remaining arguments:  []
$ collect --letter=A -n80 operand
Options:  { number: [ '80' ], letter: [ 'A' ] }
Remaining arguments:  [ 'operand' ]
$ collect --letter -n 1 -n 2 3 -- operand
Options:  { number: [ '1', '2', '3' ], letter: true }
Remaining arguments:  [ 'operand' ]
```

--------------------------------

### Start PM2 with configuration file

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/pm2.mdx

Launch your Bun application using the pm2.config.js configuration file.

```bash
pm2 start pm2.config.js
```

--------------------------------

### Paragraph Interrupted by Ordered List Starting with 1

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/md/spec.txt

This example shows that an ordered list starting with '1.' *can* interrupt a paragraph in CommonMark. The text before '1.' becomes a paragraph, and the line starting with '1.' initiates an ordered list, potentially leading to unintended results but preventing most spurious list captures.

```markdown
The number of windows in my house is
1.  The number of doors is 6.
```

```html
<p>The number of windows in my house is</p>
<ol>
<li>The number of doors is 6.</li>
</ol>
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize and Manage a React App with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/react.mdx

Use these commands to create a new React project, run it in development with hot reloading, build for production, and start the production server.

```bash
# Create a new React app
bun init --react

# Run the app in development mode
bun dev

# Build as a static site for production
bun run build

# Run the server in production
bun start
```

--------------------------------

### Force Bun Secrets API Keyring Setup via Environment Variable

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/js/bun/secrets-ci-setup.md

This command demonstrates how to explicitly force the `Bun.secrets` API test to perform its keyring setup routine, even if automatic detection might not trigger it. This is achieved by setting the `FORCE_KEYRING_SETUP` environment variable.

```bash
FORCE_KEYRING_SETUP=1 bun test test/js/bun/secrets.test.ts
```

Setup commands for creating a new NAPI project and adding the necessary Rust crate for Bun native plugins.

```bash
bun add -g @napi-rs/cli
napi new
```

```bash
cargo add bun-native-plugin
```

### Create Project from GitHub Repository

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/templating/create.mdx

Use these commands to download and initialize a project from a GitHub repository. The repository name will be used as the destination folder name.

```bash
bun create <user>/<repo>
bun create github.com/<user>/<repo>
```

--------------------------------

### Get high-precision timing with Bun.nanoseconds

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/runtime/utils.mdx

Returns the number of nanoseconds elapsed since the current Bun process started as a number. Useful for benchmarking and high-precision timing measurements.

```TypeScript
Bun.nanoseconds();
// => 7288958
```

--------------------------------

### Spawn Multi-File Bun Process in Test

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/test/AGENTS.md

Shows how to set up a multi-file test environment using `tempDir` to create temporary files and then spawn a Bun process to execute one of them.

```ts
import { bunEnv, bunExe, tempDir } from "harness";
import { test, expect } from "bun:test";

test("multi-file test spawns a Bun process", async () => {
  // If a test MUST use multiple files:
  using dir = tempDir("my-test-prefix", {
    "my.fixture.ts": `
      import { foo } from "./foo.ts";
      foo();
    `,
    "foo.ts": `
      export function foo() {
        console.log("Hello, world!");
      }
    `,
  });

  await using proc = Bun.spawn({
    cmd: [bunExe(), "my.fixture.ts"],
    env: bunEnv,
    cwd: String(dir),
  });

  const [stdout, stderr, exitCode] = await Promise.all([

    // ReadableStream in Bun supports:
    //  - `await stream.text()`
    //  - `await stream.json()`
    //  - `await stream.bytes()`
    //  - `await stream.blob()`
    proc.stdout.text(),
    proc.stderr.text(),

    proc.exitCode,
  ]);

  expect(stdout).toBe("Hello, world!");
  expect(stderr).toBe("");
  expect(exitCode).toBe(0);

```

--------------------------------

### Start and stop an API server using Bun test lifecycle hooks

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/test/lifecycle.mdx

This example illustrates how to use `beforeAll` and `afterAll` hooks to manage the lifecycle of an API server for Bun tests. The server is started once before all tests and stopped once after all tests, providing a stable testing environment for API interactions.

```typescript
import { beforeAll, afterAll } from "bun:test";
import { startServer, stopServer } from "./server";

let server;

beforeAll(async () => {
  // Start test server
  server = await startServer({
    port: 3001,
    env: "test",
  });
});

afterAll(async () => {
  // Stop test server
  await stopServer(server);
});
```

--------------------------------

### Build HTML for Production with CLI

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/html-static.mdx

Use the Bun CLI to build and minify an HTML entrypoint for production, outputting to a specified directory.

```bash
bun build ./index.html --minify --outdir=dist
```

--------------------------------

### Filter `bun install` operations by package name or path

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/pm/filter.mdx

This example demonstrates how to use the `--filter` flag with `bun install` to manage dependencies for specific packages in a monorepo. It shows filtering by excluding a package, including packages by path, and combining filters to target specific sets of packages.

```bash
# Install dependencies for all workspaces except `pkg-c`
bun install --filter '!pkg-c'

# Install dependencies for packages in `./packages` (`pkg-a`, `pkg-b`, `pkg-c`)
bun install --filter './packages/*'

# Save as above, but exclude the root package.json
bun install --filter '!./' --filter './packages/*'
```

--------------------------------

### Install Bun using Scoop

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/installation.mdx

Install Bun on Windows using the Scoop package manager. Make sure Scoop is installed and set up.

```bash
scoop install bun
```

--------------------------------

### Redirect image imports with onResolve plugin

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/plugins.mdx

Example plugin that redirects all imports starting with 'images/' to './public/images/' using onResolve(). Demonstrates filter-based matching and path transformation during module resolution.

```typescript
import { plugin } from "bun";

plugin({
  name: "onResolve example",
  setup(build) {
    build.onResolve({ filter: /.*/, namespace: "file" }, args => {
      if (args.path.startsWith("images/")) {
        return {
          path: args.path.replace("images/", "./public/images/"),
        };
      }
    });
  },
});
```

--------------------------------

### Start Qwik Development Server with Bun

Source: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/guides/ecosystem/qwik.mdx

This command starts the development server for the Qwik application using Bun. It typically utilizes Vite to provide a local server with hot-module replacement, allowing developers to see changes in real-time. The server usually becomes accessible at `http://localhost:5173`.

```sh
bun run dev
```
